content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Need a suggestion for a feel good movie

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Heyy all ,  I am crazy movie nerd and have watched a lot and alot of movies
Currenrly I am looking for some feel good movies , bored af and need something to cheer me up. Any good rom com is also appreciated, I want to watch something that makes u feel at home , releases ur anxiety,  freshens you up.

I don't care about language as long as its on netflix or prime video and has English subtitles, any suggestions are appreciated thank you !!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chef (2014)
2. Amelie! It always brings a smile to my face
3. Raising Arizona 🤩🤩🤩
4. The Birdcage
  - YES to this!!

This movie is a lovely warm hug. And so funny.
5. Some of my favorite feel good movies:

Outsourced (2006)

Tortilla Soup (2001)

High Fidelity (2000)

Safe Men (1998) 

That Thing You Do (1996)

The Birdcage (1996)

Mr. Wonderful (1993)

Her Alibi (1989)

Desperately Seeking Susan (1985)

Breaking Away (1979)

The Pink Panther (1963)

Marty (1955)
6. Babe
7. Almost Famous (2000)
8. The Family Man
9. Crocodile Dundee (Netflix)

## 2. Need suggestions for Horror Comedy movies please - one of the most underrated major genres. Both English & Foreign language movies welcome!

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Among the major genres, Horror Comedy might be the most slept on, and the most difficult to balance. Have been through most of the popular Hollywood ones, my favourites - Screams, Tremors, Tucker & Dale, Living Dead - all being masterpie. Even rarer in shows, Santa Clarita probably nailed it (screw you Netflix!).

Done with the common ones - Zombieland, Freaky, Gremlins, Witching & Bitching, Abigail, Witches, Arachnophobia, Fright Night, Bubba Ho-Tep, Lost Boys, Day Shift. Not a fan of the cheesy 80s ones. Foreign language movies do it justice as well - Bollywood (Bhool Bhulaiya, Stree), Japanese (One Cut of the Dead, Happiness of the Katikuris), European (Dead Snow, The Trip, Day of the Beast). I tried a few 80s Chinese ones (Mr. Vampire, A Chinese Ghost Story, Encounter of the Spooky Kind) but they end up being too cheesy and over the top.

Welcoming any suggestions from fellow redditors on this genre. Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What We Do In the Shadows, movie and show. For something really twisted and over the top, Visitor Q.
2. -Tucker and Dale Vs Evil

-Hot Fuzz

-Hobo With a Shotgun

-Fathers Day

Plus a lot of Troma's movies are funny like Terror Firmer, Poultrygeist, and the toxic Avenger.
3. Slither

Evil Dead 2 and Army of Darkness

The Burbs
4. Ready or Not
5. Werewolves Within

Bodies Bodies Bodies

Once Bitten
6. The Cabin in the Woods
7. Shaun of the dead?
8. Warm Bodies
9. What We Do in the Shadows - a New Zealand movie about a share house of Vampires - I just loved it!
10. You’ve already got a lot of the good ones but I enjoyed The Blackening (2022)

## 3. Judgment at Nuremberg

**Score:** 26
**Body:** Made in the 1960s but set in 1948, this movie starring Spencer Tracy and Burt Lancaster tells the story of one of the Nuremberg trials, in which four judges were accused of crimes based on their participation in the Nazi regime. It examines how normal people, or even exceptional people, can become part of a system of brutality and torture. 

It's about three hours long, deeply thoughtful and considered, and a film everyone should see. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That movie hits different when you realize how relevant those themes still are today. Spencer Tracy was incredible in it too, really sold the moral weight of the whole thing
  - Yep, we just watched it a few days ago after having not seen it in years. Burt Lancaster was also incredible in a difficult role. 

You know the feeling when something is so relevant it's depressing? That's this.
2. It's incredible. Four powerhouse performances from Maximilian Schell defending the indefensible, Burt Lancaster in his least Burt Lancaster role ever, Judy Garland back to acting for the first time in a decade and Montgomery Clift bringing so much shame and humility to his role. Plus brilliant camerawork which makes a stodgy courtroom drama really dynamic. It was also the first time for a lot of people seeing the brutality of the camps - there was quite a battle over the certificate in the same way that there was for Schindler's List.
  - I kept thinking what a complex thing it must have been for Marlene Dietrich to play her role in this film. What must have been in her mind, playing the woman whose choices were so opposite to Dietrich's own.
    - Exactly.
Same for Werner Klemperer.


Many actors who despised the Nazis were most often the best at portraying them.
Add Conrad Veidt.
3. Going to rewatch this very soon to get the most recent Nuremberg movie out of my system.
4. What impressed me was that they didn’t go for the obvious targets (like the recent Nuremberg movie). It focuses on the judges and jurists who undermined the law and protections of the people that allowed the regime to do whatever it wanted. 

It’s hugely relevant today and resonates with what the current SCOTUS has become.
5. Completely agree! Also watch Memory of Justice by Marcel Ophuls.
6. Great film, unlike the recent Nuremberg, which was terribly acted, scripted and paced imo.
7. Really, really good film. Long, dense, but perfectly executed.

## 4. Recommendations for someone who’s watched an embarrassingly low amount of movies!

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I’m a 27 year old British male (if this makes any difference to recommendations) and somehow have managed to go through my life having watched a really low amount of movies (despite working in a cinema in my teens) 

I’ve made it a new years resolution to change that but I don’t really know where to start or how to look. 

The only thing I know for certain I won’t enjoy as a genre is horror. 

I’d love to know what are the MUST WATCH and more obscure cult classic movies you guys would recommend to someone who knows nothing about anything to do with movies. 

Any recommendations would be greatly appreciated. 

(I know for certain I would like history based, based on true events, political and cultural drama types but I am open to anything apart from horror!) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mate you gotta start with the classics - Goodfellas, Shawshank Redemption, Pulp Fiction are absolute must-watches that'll hook you instantly. For history stuff try Saving Private Ryan or The Departed, and if you want something a bit different check out In Bruges since you're British you'll probably love the dark humor
2. Withnail & I

It’s a cult black comedy about two British out-of-work actors in their late 20s. The script and acting performances are second to none, endlessly quotable.
3. Forest Gump

Tremors

Rocky

Life of Pi

Slumdog Millionaire 
 
The Green Mile

1917
4. THEY LIVE!
5. If your British? 

This Is England.  It's about the rise in racist skinheads in the UK
6. The Third Man
7. Get Carter (1971) (Gangster Crime Drama) Directed by Mike Hodges. Cast: Michael Caine, Ian Hendry, John Osborne and Britt Ekland.
8. The Jerk
9. The Blues Brothers
10. All the President's Men

Lawrence of Arabia

12 Angry Men

It's a Wonderful Life

The Good, the Bad and the Ugly

There Will Be Blood (All PTA films actually)

A Hard Day's Night

Cornetto Trilogy by Edgar Wright

Babylon

## 5. Fun over the top playful movies like Austin Powers

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Austin powers just oozes charisma and swag in every line, looking for movies with similarly over the top absurd awesome quirky main characters

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I changed the flair to the correct one. Make sure to read our [guidelines](https://www.reddit.com/r/MovieSuggestions/comments/8iamsh) before posting :)
2. Wayne's World
3. Walk hard!
4. Airplane! ✈️
5. Zoolander

Blades of Glory
6. Black dynamite
7. Josie and the Pussycats
8. Ace Ventura Pet Detective
9. Barb and Starr go to Vista Del Mar
10. Johnny English

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. The best show of all time of HBO? What do you say

**Score:** 4109
**Body:** Chernobyl

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. take your pick: 

\- true detective season 1

\- the wire

\- sopranos
  - Those plus Band of brothers
2. There is no best. It's all subjective.

We had:

The Sopranos 

The Wire

Band of Brothers 

Succession

Deadwood

Game of thrones early seasons

True Detective S1

Boardwalk empire 

Westworld s1+s2
  - Great list but youre missing Rome
    - #HE WAS A CONSUL OF ROME
3. I feel like people are too afraid to admit, Game of Thrones seasons 1-4 are up there.
  - I know the sub I’m in, Game of Thrones wasn’t a masterpiece if you want a perfect score across all seasons. 

What it does have is the scale and world building that none of these other shows can match. They created such a believable world, a stupidly large number of parallel plot lines that all seem to draw you in, for an absurd amount of screen time. 

The destination was far from perfect, but the journey Game of Thrones takes you on is something I have yet to see replicated in any TV show ever.
4. Still gotta go with The Wire, but this was an awesome series.
5. Season 1 of True Detective takes the cake for me.
6. Deadwood

## 2. What is your favorite threat or warning delivered in a movie? My pick is Liam Neeson’s threat in Taken.

**Score:** 331

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "All right, I'm coming out. Any man I see out there, I'm gonna shoot him. Any sumbitch takes a shot at me, I'm not only gonna kill him, but I'm gonna kill his wife, all his friends, and burn his damn house down...You better bury Ned right!... Better not cut up, nor otherwise harm no whores... or I'll come back and kill every one of you sons of bitches."

-William Munny, Unforgiven
  - "You just shot an unarmed man!"  "He shoulda armed himself, decorating his saloon with my friend."
2. Well i was gonna say the Liam Neeson line, but thats already Taken.
3. John is a man of focus, commitment, sheer will... something you know very little about. I once saw him kill three men in a bar... with a pencil, with a fucking pencil. Then suddenly one day he asked to leave. It's over a woman, of course. So I made a deal with him. I gave him an impossible task. A job no one could have pulled off. The bodies he buried that day laid the foundation of what we are now. And then my son, a few days after his wife died, you steal his car and kill his fucking dog.
4. "You die first, get it? Your friends might get me in a rush, but not before I make your head into a canoe, you understand me?" - Tombstone
5. "If you ever disrespect my wife again, I will end you" - Robin Williams' character in Good Will Hunting. Something about the delivery of that line made it hit hard.
  - Throw in “got that chief”.
6. "Im not locked in here with you, you're locked in here with me!"- rorschach
7. Hello. My name is Inigo Montoya. You killed my father. Prepare to die
8. My name is Maximus Decimus Meridius, commander of the armies of the North. General of the Felix Legions. Loyal servant to the true emperor, Marcus Aurelius.
Father to a murdered son, husband to a murdered wife. And I will have my vengeance, in this life or the next.

## 3. What’s one movie that had a sequel nobody asked for?

**Score:** 294
**Body:** Greenland 2.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I actually don't mind the premise for Greenland 2, a lot of disaster movies end and you never get to see what happens after the world has 'ended'. I think this is an interesting premise and will probably watch it to see if it's any good. But it likely won't be good.
2. Gladiator
  - That movie didn't have a sequel. You must be mistaken. No one would sully such a fine achievement with such a cheap money grab more than two decades later.
  - I first tought "Gladiator II" is either a joke or the result of some cheap low-budget label trying to get money with a cheesy trash-grenade and a expensive title but then I saw RIDLEY SCOTT as a director.

When I think of Scott and how he is running in circles not knowing wtf he is supposed to do \[that crap he pulled off with the whole Prometheus/Alien franchise where even himself obviously didn´t really knew wtf he is actually doing - making a movie NOT being based in the alien-universe while making it look like as if it does and then all of the sudden making it being a part of it with the Prometheus-Sequel\] it finally looks like he really lost it with Gladiator II.
3. Coming to America.
  - Is that the subtitle to Greenland 2?
4. Home Alone 3 and the other poorly done sequels that came after it
5. Now you see me.
6. The Matrix Resurrections
7. Wasn’t the first one pretty conclusive? Why the hell are they making a sequel?

## 4. A fine actress who has yet to win an academy award after multiple nominations. I loved Saoirse Ronan's work in her early roles of Hanna (2011) and Atonement (2007).

**Score:** 270
**Body:** Is it her Irish accent, beautiful looks or her fine performances? She has a great screen presence and does get your attention. The pictures are from her movies Hanna, Brooklyn, Ladybird, Little Women and Atonement.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ladybird is a great film
2. Hanna is so great, and I love the score by The Chemical Brothers.
  - Hell yeah. I love the movie and their score. https://youtu.be/eE4_XlnNCcs?si=Qi6PvYJMy0p8GgaM
3. Love her as Jo in Little woman so much but damn Briony. Never hated a child so much (which means she did great)
4. Saoirse Ronan is one of the finest actors in the world.
5. The Outrun surprised me. She is really good. Hanna is such a great fun film.
6. I think she was fantastic in Byzantium. Her and Gemma Arterton both made the film extremely memorable. 
7. It’s probably cause they can’t pronounce her name correctly
8. I love her in the Talking Heads music video [Psycho Killer.](https://youtu.be/CJ54eImz88w?si=9uLe6VAwju-QyhG6)
9. Surprised she hasn’t won one tbh, especially for Brooklyn

## 5. What are some movies/shows you feel were made at the precise right time in history?

**Score:** 88
**Body:** I’ve picked LOTR trilogy.

It was a great source of comfort for people after what happened in New York September 2001, had it been made earlier the special effects wouldn’t have looked that great and had it been made later it would have use to much CGI(look at the Hobbit movies).

Not mention given how genre hungry studios are today there might have been a risk the books would have split into several movies like what happened to the Hobbit.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 1999 The Matrix
  - Oh yeah, I think this should be considered the top answer.
2. LOTR was never late, not was it early. It arrived precisely when it meant to.
3. Back to the Future
  - A movie that is somehow is both the most 80s movie ever and the most timeless movie ever.
4. X-men sort of kicked off the MCU
  - Speaking of MCU, Iron Man ACTUALLY kicked it off. At any other point in time, Im not sure if it would have worked as well, let alone the MCU becoming what it was.
5. Spider-Man 2, The Dark Knight, Avengers Endgame
6. Everything I was a fan of when I was young
7. Spiderman 1 came out at the right time especially after 9/11

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Anyone else seen Afternoons of Solitude?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Don't see much discussion for this documentary, watched it last night.  
I want to preface this by saying I didn't know much about bullfighting other than images of matadors waving the red fabric getting bulls to run through it. I had no idea that they weakened the bulls before the fight and killed the bulls at the climax, and how barbaric and brutal it was. I was brought to tears at several points during the film watching these animals die, and it really shows it. The film does not shy away from the brutality of it at all, it literally shows the light leaving these animals eyes as they draw their last breaths. Extremely bloody.

It doesn't take a stance on it's subject, nor does it try to tell you how to feel about it... it just presents the bullfights and some moments in-between of the matador and his entourage after a bullfight in their van, driving back to the hotel and praising his performance ("you have the biggest balls", "you are the greatest" totally glazing him, he doesn't respond much to this and seems to be in a state of contemplation as they are complimenting him), and him getting prepared in his hotel room before a bullfight. There are no interviews, barely any dialogue. It doesn't tell you anything about his personal life or anything about him, and during the bullfights it's very closeup, you hear cheers from the audience but you barely see any of them in the film.

I can see someone being totally fucking disgusted and not wanting anything to do with this film. I've seen someone call it two hours of animal abuse, and I dont disagree...But honestly I can see someone also seeing this as an animal rights movie BECAUSE it shows the inhumanity of it all, the kind of toxic machismo mindset these guys operate with. But at the same time I don't feel like the film is condemning it either. It's just plain showing it. There was this kind of purity and honesty in this that was refreshing to me.

This also honestly has some of the most arresting visuals I've ever seen in any piece of media ever. This totally eclipses everything I've seen from 2025 (still have a ton to catch up on) and is probably the best thing I've seen this decade so far. I've never seen anything quite like it.

Sorry this is so rambly and all over the place, I'm not a good writer at all.  
If you think you can stomach it, I highly recommend it.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Haven’t seen it, but it sounds pretty similar to Hemingway’s Death in the Afternoon, although Hemingway is really writing a love letter to something he knows is going to die.

He talks as much about how dangerous it is for the matadors as for the bulls, but not as a false equivalence, just as a simple fact.
2. >This totally eclipses everything I've seen from 2025 (still have a ton to catch up on) and is probably the best thing I've seen this decade so far. I've never seen anything quite like it.

It's my favourite film of 2024, top 3 of the decade. You should also check out Serra's Pacifiction, if you haven't already. 

  
I will repost my post about it that I made on r/criterion defending the film, so it's a bit scattershot. 



​There not being any commentary from the director is what makes it interesting, there's no intervention, no opinion. You're presented by something and have to make up your own mind, instead of being told to think X, Y, Z. I mean if you compare it the latest Herzog - White Elephants, that one is basically a slightly elevated NatGeo doc.

When I saw the film at a film festival, during both Q&As they were people violently against the main character saying he's completely pathetic and then they were people waxing lyrical about how he moves like a dancer and is so poetic etc. I think there are many many contradictions and ambiguities without handholding the viewer, which is rare. He's displayed as both a pathetic wannabe macho guy with masculinity issues (like during the jokes and also excessive ball talk), but he's also portrayed as somebody who's an artist, kind of like a ballet dancer and who cares about his craft.

Serra has been interested in mixing the real and the artificial in all of his fiction work, which is why he shoots with long takes, lets the actors improvise, feeds them lines to throw them off. In short he wants a combination of the character he created, but also the real person behind the actor. Which leads to some strange, awkward effects. This is a perfect subject for him, because he deals with something extremely real and tangible, but the subject matter is also ornate and baroque. So there's something real like the bull and death, but also extremely performative and artificial like the clothing and the whole performance. I like this tension, I think it's the closest he has ever come to achieve this kind of artificial reality, that he has tried to, well along with Pacifiction. There're many various contradictions that make the whole really intriguing.

Beyond that the style is pretty unique, it's all claustrophobic, there's only one shot of the outside, when they go with a car, through a windshield. You never see the spectators, it's just the sand, very atavistic. It's also interesting in how it approaches the topic of watching, like his previous 2 films.
3. I appreciate the way the film looks and style of shooting. With that said, I think bullfighting is a joke, and I have zero respect for bullfighters. If the documentary is made to make me feel less of the ‘sport’ and the players who dub themselves warriors, it succeeds.
4. I’m about halfway through, and I do indeed think the filmmaker is making a stance on bullfighting. To your point, it does not gloss over the indifferent brutality of it all. No reasonable person would watch this and come away thinking “yea bullfighting is cool”. 

I don’t think I’ve hated someone as much as the central character. 

There is no glory. They “fight” a bull that is 75% dead. You can see in the opening scene where the bull is a bit too spirited, so they pause the fight to throw a few spears into it. 

I’m glad there’s someone else out there to talk about this doc — I share in your disgust OP. I imagine you, like me, found yourself rooting for the bull when he gets tossed around mid-fight.

I also found it cringey and bizarre with how much praise they threw at him. And his stupid faces he made while fighting the bull.
  - Matadors are rock stars in Spain
  - >No reasonable person would watch this and come away thinking “yea bullfighting is cool”.

Well you would be surprised. I saw the film twice at a film festival and during the Q&As and there were people praising the performance of the main bullfighter. 

Albert Serra himself doesn't say that it's anti-bullfighting film for a good reason. If you listen to interviews with him, he definitely admires the main character in certain ways. 

That's what makes the film interesting in my opinion, he says he has no opinion, and he probably hasn't about bullfighting as a whole, but he puts in many contradictory things in the film that you can interpret how you want.

## 2. Fool's Paradise by Charlie Day

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I just watched Fool's Paradise a few hours ago, and I honestly thought it was great. I can’t really wrap my head around the bad ratings it received. It made me question the entire internet rating system.

I really enjoyed how it takes a premise, sticks with it, and never loses its grip. It’s definitely going on my rewatch list.

If I have any critique, it would be that it didn’t lean into the Chaplin-ness quite enough. 

Take the dancing scene for example, I would have loved to see way more homage to old movies. Or having some scene referencing logs stuck on the rails of a moving train, or a house falling down only for the character to survive because he’s standing exactly where the window lands. More early 20th-century movie fan service.

My rating: 7/10 for normies, 8/10 for film aficionados.



### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Looking at IMDB, it seemed that it's not just the critics that didn't like it, many members of the audience didn't like it, either. I skimmed the comments and there is a lot that is criticized -- pacing, aimlessness, shallowness of character, and a story that's stretched out way past its own gimmick.

What are some other comedies that you find comparable to this film?
  - Non really, it’s quite a unique film.

It’s not so out there that regular audiences would feel alienated or anything, but also it’s definitely not main stream.

If you force me to pick something thou, I’d say “The invention of lying” by Ricky Gervais.

What you say about the critics online is correct, I just think they are all wrong. It’s a charming movie.
    - Watch Being There, it was a big influence (Charlie has said so, I forget where) and is a way better movie.
      - Man, Being There is so so good.
    - I think if you liked Fool’s Paradise (as I did) you might like the movie The Imposters with Stanley Tucci
2. Have you ever considered that all taste is subjective, and that online rating systems are not some neutral, inalienable, authority that will perfectly align with what you might like?

---
